description: A small bush warbler with olive-green upperparts, bright yellow underparts, and a white eyebrow and throat. It lives in lowland to sub-montane forests, usually moving quickly in the undergrowth. Often found in small groups with other species. Its song is a short, melodious series of high whistles ending on the highest note. The species is commonly found in its range.

A medium-sized raptor from Indonesia, Papua New Guinea, and the Solomon Islands. Recently split from the Grey Goshawk, it is widespread and fairly common even in degraded habitats. Very little is known about its breeding, diet, or behavior. Highly variable, with 22 subspecies, some experts suggest it may represent multiple species.

A medium-sized kingfisher (22–25.5 cm, 60–90 g) found in Brunei, Indonesia, Malaysia, Myanmar, and Thailand. It lives in tropical lowland and montane forests but is threatened by habitat loss. The bird is blue-green above with yellow spots on the back, a green crown, and a black eye mask. Some individuals show a rufous breast band, while others have entirely rufous underparts.

A small passerine bird from the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia, found in scrub, woodland, and cultivated areas. Breeding males are black above with bright yellow underparts and white wing bars, while non-breeding males and females are yellow-green above with black-and-white wings. Males display in flight by fluffing their feathers and spiraling in the air.

A medium-sized sunbird, fairly common across much of its range, found in gardens, farmland, and open forest edges. Males are bright red with a dark grey belly, and a shiny blue crown and moustache stripe, while females are dull olive-yellow with paler underparts. It feeds mainly on nectar but also takes insects, often perching to feed though capable of hovering. It has been unofficially recognized as the national bird of Singapore.

A medium-sized sunbird found in montane forests up to 1800 m in Borneo, Sumatra, Malaysia, and southwest Thailand. It is most often seen feeding on flowers, especially mistletoes. Males are bright crimson with a white belly, long red tail, and dark moustache stripe, while females are plain with a greyish wash on the head, white underparts, and faint pale brown edges on the wings and tail.

A small, plain brown bird with a grey head and horn-grey bill, lacking distinctive markings. Found in Brunei, Indonesia, Malaysia, and Thailand, it inhabits lowland and foothill forests, and less commonly montane forests at higher elevations. Usually active in the lower and middle forest strata, often in small groups or mixed flocks. The species is threatened by habitat loss.

A plain fulvetta (14–15.5 cm) found in Cambodia, Laos, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam, inhabiting subtropical or tropical montane forests. Northern subspecies have dull olive-green upperparts, a bold black line above the eye, and gray-washed underparts, while southern subspecies are richer brown above with a whiter belly. Despite its name, it does not always occur at higher altitudes than similar species.

A colorful, easily recognizable songbird found on the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo. It has a bright lemon-yellow belly, snowy white throat, bluish-grey head, and greenish upperparts, lacking a crest. Inhabits lowland and hill forests, often seen in mixed-species flocks.

A medium-sized, chicken-like waterbird found across South and Southeast Asia, common in grasslands, marshes, river edges, drains, gardens, and farmland. Adults have dark slate upperparts, white face, breast, and belly, with a rufous patch under the tail; juveniles are similar but duller. Often seen foraging in open areas and near human habitation.

A medium-sized, mostly black hornbill found in Brunei, Indonesia, Malaysia, Myanmar, and Thailand, inhabiting lowland and foothill tropical forests. It has a glossy black body, a two-toned tail, and pale facial skin; males have fully black bills, while females have pale brown bill tips. Fairly social, often foraging in medium-sized groups, and commonly seen in dense forest as well as openings like clearings or river edges.

A small flycatcher found in Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, Thailand, and Vietnam, inhabiting subtropical and tropical montane forests. It has a bright white throat with strong rufous-orange markings on the eyebrow and forehead. Feeds actively near the ground in low vegetation, gleaning from leaves and catching insects in short flights.

A large, black hornbill found in Brunei, Indonesia, Malaysia, Singapore, and Thailand, inhabiting old lowland forests. Males have heavy ivory-colored bills, sometimes with bright white eyebrow markings or entirely black heads, while females have darker bills and reddish facial skin with fainter brow markings. Usually seen alone, in pairs, or small groups, typically in the lower canopy compared to larger hornbills.

description: A medium-sized sunbird with a relatively short bill, common across much of Southeast Asia in open forests, forest edges, and urban areas. Males have a brown face, reddish throat, and greenish-blue crown and back, while females are plainer with yellow underparts, brownish-grey head, and faint eye-rings.

A small, active nectar-feeding bird with a long, slightly curved bill, found in moist forests of South and Southeast Asia. It has an olive back, lemon-yellow underparts, and a pale grey-white facial pattern. Inhabits the lower strata of secondary forests, forest edges, plantations, and gardens. Feeds on nectar, especially from banana flowers, and also consumes other insects.

A large spiderhunter found in tropical and subtropical hill forests. It has a long, curved black bill, yellow-green upperparts with black streaks, and lighter yellow underparts. Feeds on invertebrates from foliage and nectar from flowers. Often moves in mixed-species flocks.

A cryptic, nocturnal bird found in lowland and foothill forests up to 800 m in Brunei, Cambodia, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, Thailand, and Vietnam. Males have broad wing bars, whitish underparts, and irregular white spots on the chest, while females are lighter brown, almost rufous, with fewer bars and a wide “necklace” of white spots. In Indonesia, its range overlaps with other frogmouth species, but it is the only frogmouth across most of its distribution.

A cryptic, nocturnal frogmouth found in lowland and foothill forests. Both sexes are similar, with a bright rufous morph showing scattered white spots on the wings and scaled white underparts, and a darker brown morph with the same general pattern. Rarely seen, it can be distinguished from other frogmouth species by its overall pattern and lower-elevation habitat.

A large hornbill (83-102 cm) of the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo, inhabiting lowland and medium-altitude rainforest below about 900 m, occasionally using fruit, oil-palm, and rubber plantations. Males are white on the head, neck, breast, and tail with an erect white crest, and black elsewhere; females are almost entirely black except for the white crest, and both sexes show bare dark-blue skin between the eye and bill. It is territorial and feeds on fruit, lizards, arthropods, and larvae; the breeding female seals herself into a tree-hole nest and is fed through a narrow opening by the male and a cooperative group.

A dark maroon woodpecker with a glossy yellow bill and grey-brown head and underparts, found in Brunei, Indonesia, Malaysia, southern Myanmar, Singapore, and southern Thailand. Females generally have paler heads than males. Inhabits lowland and montane forests, foraging low or near the ground, often on fallen trunks and dense undergrowth. Often joins mixed-species flocks.
A small songbird in the bulbul family with a black head, found in forests of Southeast Asia. Commonly inhabits tropical and subtropical forests across its range.

A small ground-dwelling chat found in dense vegetation, usually near streams. Both sexes are similar, overall brown or greyish with white eyebrows and throat. Secretive and difficult to see, it inhabits forest undergrowth in its Southeast Asian range.

A small dark chat found in the undergrowth and forest floor of montane rainforests in Java. Males are dark grey overall, slightly paler on the belly, while females have dark grey head and chest, rufous-brown back, wings, and rump, and paler brown-orange underparts. Both sexes have thin white eyebrows and long black legs. Feeds on the ground and on low fallen branches.

A large, striking hornbill found in lowland and foothill forests up to 1,400 m in Borneo, Sumatra, Java, the Malay Peninsula, Singapore, and southern Thailand. It has glossy black plumage with a white tail marked by a dark band, and a large bright orange bill with a prominent casque, short and rectangular in females, long and upward-curving in males. Often seen flying strongly over the forest, usually in pairs or small groups.

A small and slender cuckoo with orange underparts, a grey head, and brown back and tail. A hepatic form shows rufous-brown upperparts and whitish underparts with dark barring. It inhabits open woodland, forest edges, and agricultural areas. Native to Asia, from India and Nepal to China and Indonesia.

A medium-sized cuckoo with bright orange or yellowish-orange breast and belly. Western populations (Southeast Asia to Sulawesi) have yellow eye-rings and plainer tails, while eastern populations (Maluku, eastern Lesser Sundas, Papua, and Australia) show grey eye-rings and more heavily barred tails. In the west the orange extends to the throat, separating it from the plaintive cuckoo, while in the east the grey eye-ring distinguishes it from the fan-tailed cuckoo.

A slender brown cuckoo with broad fine barring, pale face, and whitish underparts. It has rufous upperparts with regular dark bands and a whitish underside with fine striations, often showing a white eyebrow. Found in lowland and foothill forests, usually perching unobtrusively in foliage, though males may sing from exposed branches in the breeding season. Distributed across the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia.

A small, bright green bird with a distinctive umbrella-shaped head. Males have black wings and a dark comma mark behind the eye, while females are paler green and lack dark markings. It inhabits lowland forests, overlapping in Borneo with other green broadbills that occur at higher elevations. Compared to similar species, females lack black, wing markings are clear bars rather than spots, and the bird is overall larger. Belongs to the family Calyptomenidae, formerly placed in Eurylaimidae.

description: A large, vivid green broadbill with heavy black mottling and streaking. Males show black on the throat and underparts, while females are smaller and lack black markings. Endemic to the montane forests of north-central Borneo, usually at 900–1,700 m. Found singly, in pairs, or in small groups, often around fruiting trees.

A nocturnal bird with intricate grey-brown plumage, bright brown ear patches, and black, chestnut, and brown-barred wings. Shows prominent white tail corners in flight. Usually rests on the ground or low branches in open forest, secondary growth, plantations, gardens, and even under streetlights while foraging. Widespread from the Himalayan foothills through South and Southeast Asia to northern Australia, inhabiting lowland, montane, and mangrove forests.

A large terrestrial cuckoo with glossy greenish-purple wings, long dark tail, finely barred upperparts, black head, and pale greenish-blue face and bill. It forages on the ground, often following ant columns or wild pigs to catch disturbed prey. Endemic to Borneo, where it inhabits lowland primary forests, especially swampy or riverine areas. Threatened by habitat loss, and was once considered conspecific with the Sumatran ground cuckoo.

A medium-sized, sturdy cuckoo often found in grasslands, scrub, forest edges, and plantations. Adults are black with bright chestnut wings and fine silvery streaks on the neck and shoulders. Juveniles are browner above, paler below, with duller chestnut wings and barred tails. Smaller than the greater coucal, with darker eyes and duller chestnut upperparts marked with silvery streaks. Distributed widely across marshy and shrubby habitats, it is one of the few coucals with seasonal plumage variation.

A rufous-brown coucal with a long, often ragged tail. Juveniles show paler eyes and a lighter breast and throat than adults. Found singly or in pairs in lowland and foothill forests, especially in dense tangles and forest edges. Differs from the lesser coucal by its entirely brown plumage lacking black. Endemic to Indonesia.

A burly, long-tailed coucal found in lowland forest of Brunei, the Malay Peninsula/Thailand, Sumatra, and Borneo, preferring dense lowland and foothill rainforest but also occurring in scrubby or grassy areas with heavy cover. Adults are coal-black with bright rufous wings and back; juveniles are messier-looking with extensive pale barring over much of the upperparts. It closely resembles the Greater Coucal but is smaller and shorter-tailed, and it gives sad, muffled hoots.

A very large, crow-like cuckoo with glossy black body, long thick black tail, and striking chestnut wings. Adults have dark red eyes, while juveniles are duller with white barring on the tail and underparts and variable black streaking on the wings and upperparts. Found in a wide range of habitats from forests to farmland and gardens, but generally absent from dense primary forest. Larger than the lesser coucal, with a longer bill, red eyes, and no white streaks on the wings.

Restricted to montane Java, Bali, Lombok, and Sumbawa. Like others in the Aberrant Bush Warbler complex it belongs to, it is a nondescript, olive-brown, insectivorous bird with a well-defined long supercilium and a longish tail habitually held cocked, skulking through dense, low vegetation and heard far more often than seen. Taxonomy is unsettled: Avibase currently treats it as a full species (Horornis vulcanius), while eBird/Clements/Birds of the World currently lump it within the wider-ranging Aberrant Bush Warbler.

A small bright-green leafbird, with males showing a black-blue throat and females sometimes a yellowish wash on the throat. Resembles the greater green leafbird but has a narrower black mask and a touch of yellow on the forehead, and lacks blue in the wings unlike the blue-winged and Bornean leafbirds. Found in lowland broadleaf forests, secondary growth, plantations, and large gardens, usually in the canopy and often joining mixed-species flocks. Threatened by habitat loss across its range in Southeast Asia.

The world’s smallest cuckoo, only about 15 cm long and 17 g in weight. Glossy bronze-green above with barred underparts, showing red or dark eyes depending on the subspecies. Found in lowland forests across Southeast Asia, New Guinea, and northern to eastern Australia. Some subspecies, including rufomerus and crassirostris, are sometimes treated as separate species.

Likely refers to the same species as Chrysocolaptes guttacristatus (see that entry) under an older, broader taxonomy that once covered the whole Greater Flameback complex. A large woodpecker (33 cm) with an erect crest and a golden-yellow to dark brown back, found in forests from the Himalayan foothills through the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, Java, and northeast Borneo.

A medium-sized woodpecker, moss-green overall with blood-red wings and neck, a bright yellow crest, and pale hindneck. The throat shows indistinct black-and-white checkering. Prefers mature lowland and hill forests, rarely venturing into secondary growth or open edges. Forages mainly in the mid-canopy, usually alone, in pairs, or with mixed-species flocks. Native to Southeast Asia.

A small, active sunbird common in gardens, parks, and forest edges, and the most widespread urban sunbird across much of its range. Both sexes have plain olive backs, yellow underparts, and white tail edges shown in flight. Males show a shining blue throat, while females have a yellow throat and eyebrow. Geographic variation occurs, with some males in central-eastern Indonesia showing dark underparts. Smaller and slimmer-billed than the olive-backed sunbird female, which lacks white in the tail. Also compare with females of Leptocoma sunbirds.

A member of the crow family, roughly the size of a Eurasian jay or slightly smaller, found from the lower Himalayas in northeastern India southeast through Thailand, Malaysia, Sumatra, and northwestern Borneo, in evergreen forest (including bamboo), clearings, and scrub. Wild birds are bright green (fading to turquoise in captivity or on a poor diet, since the pigment is carotenoid-based), with a thick black stripe from the bill through the eye to the nape, reddish-maroon wings, a fairly long white-tipped tail, and vivid red eye-rims, bill, and legs. It forages both on the ground and in the canopy, taking a high proportion of animal prey.

A striking shama with a broad rufous-orange tail tipped black. Males have dark gray upperparts, short white eyebrows, and rufous rump and belly, while females are browner above with paler but more extensive orange on the underparts. Found in lowland and foothill forests, favoring primary habitats and less tolerant of disturbance than other shamas. Forages near the ground or in trees. Threatened by habitat loss across its limited range in southern Thailand, Malaysia, Sumatra, and Borneo.

An elegant songbird with a long graduated tail. Males are glossy black above with bright rufous-brown underparts and white crown, rump, and undertail, while females are similar but paler. Mainly distributed in Sabah, with some hybridization with the white-rumped shama elsewhere in Borneo. Found in lowland and lower montane forests, including disturbed areas and abandoned plantations. Often trapped for the bird trade. Its varied song includes loud whistles, flute-like notes, and frequent mimicry, with a sharp “tsick” call.

A large black crow with a relatively long, slightly downcurved bill and a slimmer shape than the large-billed crow. Plumage shows a dark bluish gloss at close range. Inhabits intact lowland forests and forest edges, usually seen in pairs or small groups, often flying with deep wingbeats over roads, rivers, or forest openings. Gives harsh nasal calls typical of crows.

A large black crow with a long, dagger-like bill that curves along the upper edge. Plumage shows variable gloss across its wide range. Highly adaptable, feeding on carrion, human scraps, and food stolen from other birds, and often mobbing raptors in groups. Found in diverse habitats including forests, open woodland, farmland, and urban areas. Voice harsh and raspy, typical of crows.

A small, entirely black crow about 39 cm long with a pale iris and short tail, resembling an all-black piping crow. Endemic to the Banggai Islands off southeastern Sulawesi, Indonesia, it is now known mainly from Peleng Island, where it lives in mountain forest above 500 m elevation. Known for over a century from only two museum specimens and feared extinct, it was rediscovered in 2007-2008. The total population is estimated at around 500 mature individuals and is declining due to habitat loss.

A large, odd-looking broadbill, mostly dark with a pale breast patch and a big pink bill. Its long body and size give it a starling-like appearance, though bulkier. Hunts by sallying from perches, often foraging socially in noisy medium-sized groups. Found in lowland to montane forests, usually in wetter areas. Very vocal, giving a variety of calls including rising whistles, soft notes, and harsh rattling sounds. Native to Southeast Asia, and although affected by habitat loss, it remains widespread.

A medium-sized cuckoo, mostly gray above with white underparts barred black, while some females show a rufous “hepatic” morph with dense barring. Very similar to the Oriental and Himalayan cuckoos, but shows darker gray upperparts with stronger contrast, broader barring below, and a yellower unmarked vent. Juveniles are dark gray-brown above with white-edged feathers. More often heard than seen, inhabiting montane and foothill forests across Southeast Asia. Formerly lumped with the Oriental and Himalayan cuckoos but now treated as a distinct species.

description: A slender gray-and-white cuckoo with a loud, far-carrying four-note call. Adults closely resemble Oriental, Himalayan, and Common cuckoos but show darker back and wings with dark eyes. Juveniles are heavily patterned, often with whitish crown, collar, and wing edging. Found in forests, edges, and gardens, laying eggs in the nests of drongos, shrikes, and other hosts. Widely distributed from the Indian Subcontinent to China, Southeast Asia, and Indonesia.

A medium-sized (30-35 cm) brood-parasitic cuckoo that breeds from the Himalayas east to southern China and Taiwan, migrating to Southeast Asia and the Greater Sunda Islands for the winter. Adult males are dark ashy-grey above with a blackish, white-spotted and -tipped tail, an ash-grey chin-to-breast, and white underparts barred black; females show a rufous tinge on the breast and sometimes rufous, dark-barred upperparts. It is nearly identical in appearance to the Oriental Cuckoo, from which it was only recognized as a distinct species after a 2005 study; the two are best distinguished by voice.

A small, crested flycatcher with a gray head, narrow white eye-ring, olive upperparts, and bright yellow underparts. Active in the canopy, usually in mixed-species flocks but also alone or in pairs, foraging with flycatcher-like sallies. Commonly vocal, giving loud squeaky songs and sharp repeated calls. Found across tropical Asia in forested habitats, with several recognized subspecies. Now placed in the family Stenostiridae, the fairy flycatchers.

A small babbler of lowland and foothill rainforests in Borneo, more common in disturbed forest. Reddish-brown above with dark gray hood and chest, buff underparts, and bare blue skin around the eyes and throat, the latter swelling when calling. Forages low, often in dead foliage. Similar to the chestnut-winged babbler but distinguished by dark eyes and blue facial skin. Vocal, giving owl-like trilling songs along with harsh vibrating calls. Found in Borneo and Banggai Island, and assessed as Least Concern.

A small undergrowth babbler of lowland and foothill rainforests, including secondary growth. Chestnut-brown above with pale gray face and chest, fading to whitish belly. Lacks the blue facial skin of the similar grey-hooded babbler. Usually in pairs or small groups, foraging at mid-level in forest. Song a trilled, owl-like series, along with noisy vibrating and chattering calls. Found in the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and nearby islands, and assessed as Least Concern.

A small, attractive babbler endemic to Java and Bali, common in forest edges, abandoned gardens, and disturbed habitats. Bright rufous above with grayish underparts, the extent of reddish below varying by subspecies—birds in Bali show the strongest reddish wash. Forages mainly in the undergrowth but sometimes higher, joining mixed flocks or seen alone or in pairs. Song is a fast, soft series often given in duets, with additional dry chattering calls. Despite its restricted range, it remains widespread and is assessed as Least Concern.

A tiny brown babbler with a rufous crown, inhabiting forests and edges with dense understory from the Himalayan foothills into Southeast Asia, at 120–2,100 m. Very similar to Rufous-capped Babbler but tends to show a paler throat, grayer face, less yellow below, and fine dark crown streaks with a duller, shorter rufous patch. Vocal repertoire includes quick “chit” and “whit” calls, rolling rattles, and a song of rising piping whistles. Formerly, the Buff-chested Babbler was treated as a separate species but is now included within this species.

description: A striking broadbill of lowland rivers, mangroves, and forested streams. Glossy black above with bold white wing bars, rich maroon underparts and neck-band, and a narrow black chin stripe. Bill large and distinctive, bright blue with warm orange and yellow-green tones. Often conspicuous in breeding season when its long hanging nests are suspended above water. Voice is harsh and nasal, a series of low mechanical croaks. The sole member of its genus (Cymbirhynchus), with females slightly smaller than males. No other bird in its range matches its unique appearance.

description: A medium-sized flycatcher endemic to the lowland forests of Sumatra and Borneo. The male is ocean-blue above with a paler forehead, rump, and shoulder patches, and has orange underparts; it is distinguished from the similar Bornean Blue Flycatcher by its darker coloration and longer, slenderer bill. It inhabits subtropical/tropical moist lowland forest and is threatened by ongoing habitat loss.

A neat, bright brown flycatcher with large dark eyes, dark broad-based bill, white throat and chest contrasting with a faint brownish “vest.” Found in lowland and foothill broadleaf forests, often perching low or at mid-level, frequently flicking and fanning its tail. Voice is a series of short, thin, high-pitched notes. Occurs in Brunei, Indonesia, Malaysia, and Thailand, mainly in moist forests and swampy habitats. Classified as Near Threatened due to ongoing habitat loss.

A fairly large forest flycatcher, typically seen perched quietly at mid-level in dense woodland. Males are uniformly pale blue with a paler grayish belly. Females are brownish-gray with white throat and belly, often showing a faint rufous wash in the tail, lacking bright colors and slightly larger-bodied than similar female flycatchers. Juveniles resemble pale green-blue flycatchers but show greener-blue tones, a pale eye-ring, and a pale rump bar. Best distinguished from the lookalike verditer flycatcher by structure—shorter bill, no black mask, and subtler coloration.

A long-tailed corvid, restricted to hill and montane forests of Borneo—the only treepie on the island. Shows a thick black bill, black wings boldly marked with white, and bright tawny-brown underparts and face. Highly social and noisy, often moving in groups that keep contact through an array of grunts, rasps, and metallic ringing notes. Sometimes tame and approachable, especially along forest trails and around campsites. Sometimes considered a subspecies of the Sumatran treepie but usually treated as a distinct Bornean endemic.

A tiny, active flowerpecker, with males showing bright bluish-gray upperparts, yellow to orange underparts (varying by subspecies), and a small orange patch on the back. Females are duller but still distinctive with yellow-washed underparts and rump. Forages busily like other flowerpeckers, hovering briefly at flowers or gleaning insects from foliage. Common in forest edges, gardens, and village surroundings from lowlands up to foothills, less frequent in dense forest. Voice a mix of sharp, buzzy notes and thin calls. Widely distributed across South and Southeast Asia, including Bangladesh, India, Myanmar, Thailand, Malaysia, Brunei, Singapore, Indonesia, and the Philippines.

A striking, glossy-black drongo with a long, deeply forked tail that often curves upward at the tips. Adults have bright red eyes, while juveniles show darker eyes. Plumage shines with iridescent hues in good light. Distinguished from glossy starlings by its heavier downcurved bill and tail shape. Found in varied habitats, where it perches conspicuously and aggressively sallies after insects and small prey. Vocal repertoire is harsh and varied, including loud squawks and skillful mimicry of other birds such as butcherbirds and kingfishers. In Australia it is the only drongo, arriving in Queensland in late spring to breed in high, exposed trees.

description: A large, glossy black drongo with iridescent wings and breast, a thick curved bill, and a triangular tail with flared tips. Some subspecies show fine crest-like forehead feathers, while birds in Java and Sulawesi have white eyes. It is found alone, in small groups, or in mixed flocks across a wide range of habitats. The call is varied and harsh, with metallic, nasal, and horn-like notes.

A medium-sized, slender drongo with red eyes and gray plumage that appears nearly black in the west of its range and paler in the east, where many birds also show a distinct pale oval patch on the face. It has a long, deeply forked tail and is common in wooded areas, often sallying out in twisting flight to catch insects and aggressively chasing other birds that compete for food or nectar. Most populations migrate southward in winter. It resembles the black drongo but is slimmer and usually occurs in different habitats. The call is a mix of harsh and sweet notes, and it sometimes mimics other species, though the imitation is not very precise.

A large glossy black drongo with a backward-curving crest and long racket-shaped outer tail feathers, though young birds lack the elongated streamers. It lives in forests, often joins mixed-species flocks, and is highly vocal, producing metallic notes and expert imitations to attract attention or steal prey. Widespread across Asia, it shows notable regional variation.

description: A medium-sized glossy black drongo with bright red eyes and two long outer tail feathers ending in decorative rackets that vary in shape across its range. Juveniles are duller and lack the long streamers. It resembles the larger greater racket-tailed drongo but lacks a crest, giving the head a flat appearance, and has squarer tail tips. Found in tropical forests from mid to high elevations, it forages mostly beneath closed canopy, often joining mixed-species flocks. Its vocal range is impressive, including ringing whistles, bouncing notes, harsh nasal calls, and imitations of many other birds.

description: A small bird-of-paradise found only in the lowland and foothill forests of Waigeo and Batanta. Males are striking, with bare pale blue skin on the crown divided by thin black lines, a velvety dark green breast, crimson back, yellow collar, and a flat, circular tail with iridescent blue streamers. During courtship, the male raises its feathers into an elaborate display while calling with a rising, descending “piuu!”. Females are brown above with rufous wings and barred underparts. Readily identifiable within its limited range.

A strikingly large black woodpecker with a contrasting white belly and pale eyes. Males show a bright red crest, while females have a darker one. Its size and mostly black plumage make it unmistakable within its range. Found in tall dead trees, both standing and fallen, often near rivers, where it drums powerfully and calls with loud “kyick!” notes and long “keck” series. Usually seen alone or in pairs.

description: A large and elegant forest pigeon, mainly pale silvery gray with rich green wings and back. Some populations, such as in Sulawesi and northern Philippines, show a distinct orange-ochre patch on the nape. It inhabits lowland to foothill forests, including edges and secondary growth near villages. Very vocal, giving a deep resonant “rrruh-RUOOHH” along with varied longer or modulated calls. Widely distributed across South and Southeast Asia into Indonesia and the Philippines.

A large short-tailed parrot with a big head and heavy bill. Remarkably dimorphic: males are bright green with red underwings, while females are vivid red with purple underparts and underwings. Found in lowland forests of New Guinea, nearby islands, and northern Cape York, often flying high over the canopy in the morning while giving loud, piercing screams. Escapees or introduced birds are also seen outside the natural range.

A brilliant jewel-like pitta, shy and difficult to spot as it forages quietly on the forest floor. Best located by its distinctive long, slightly broken whistle. When seen, it shows a glowing red crown, dark purplish-blue back, and glittering blue wing coverts. It inhabits wet lowland and foothill forests with dense undergrowth, occasionally persisting in secondary or modified habitats. Found across parts of Southeast Asia from Myanmar and Thailand to Malaysia, Brunei, Indonesia, and Singapore, though threatened by ongoing habitat loss. The Sabah population is now treated separately as the black-crowned pitta.

A striking ground-dwelling pitta, endemic to Borneo’s lowland dipterocarp forests. Shows a jet-black head, dark bluish upperparts, brilliant scarlet underparts, and shining blue wing coverts, with a fine silver streak behind the eye. Closely resembles Garnet Pitta, but lacks the red crown. Despite its vivid colors, it stays low and hidden in shaded gullies and dense undergrowth, moving quietly as it forages. Best detected by its clear, rising whistle that cuts off suddenly. Described in 1877, it is confined to Borneo and does not occur elsewhere.

A peculiar, rail-like bird of the forest floor, shy and often hidden in dense lowland or foothill forest. Plumage is mostly warm brown, but the face is boldly patterned in black, white, yellow, orange, and rusty-red, with the contrasting black-and-white especially striking in shaded undergrowth. Long legs, neck, and slender bill give it a shorebird-like appearance unlike any other ground bird in its range. During courtship it inflates a violet throat sac and delivers an eerie, rising and falling whistle. Though declining due to loss of primary forest, it can still be found locally in logged or hill forests.

A scarce, forest-dwelling bulbul of the Sunda region, restricted to the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo. Plain brown overall with a short crest and a distinctive ability to raise the feathers of its upper back into a loose puffed “ruff,” giving the species its name. Typically keeps to the midstory of lowland and foothill rainforests, where it moves quietly in pairs or small groups. Like many forest specialists, it is vulnerable to deforestation and ongoing loss of primary habitat.

A large bird (~21.5–23 cm) with striking purple, yellow, and black plumage. It inhabits subtropical and tropical moist lowland forests. Found across Southeast Asia, including Indonesia, Malaysia, and neighboring countries. Currently considered a common species, not endangered.

A small bird with a black head, breastband, and upperparts, contrasted by a white neckband and yellow streaking on the back and wings. Its underparts are wine-pink fading to yellow, with a bright blue beak edged in black. Females differ slightly with an incomplete black breastband. Inhabits tropical forests of Southeast Asia and is generally considered a common species

A small bird from the Muscicapidae family with a distinctive rufous-colored chest. It inhabits subtropical and tropical moist lowland and montane forests. Found in Brunei, Indonesia, Malaysia, and Thailand. Currently considered a threatened species due to habitat loss.

A very small flycatcher with a large head, big eyes, and short tail. Males are blue above with a white eyebrow and bright orange throat, while females are brown with reddish wings and pale eye-rings. It inhabits dense forests and bamboo thickets at higher elevations, sometimes reaching foothills. Generally considered a common species.

A medium-sized bird; males have bold black-and-white markings, while females are dull brownish-gray with pale underparts and a reddish rump. It breeds in broadleaf, coniferous, and mixed forests of foothills and mountains. Often perches openly to catch prey and may join mixed flocks outside the breeding season. Currently regarded as a common species.

A stocky, short-tailed parrot with predominantly green plumage. Males show red cheeks with a mauve nape and crown, while females are duller with a brown head. Found in Indonesia, Papua New Guinea, and northern Australia. Considered a common species with 17 recognized subspecies.

A small grayish-brown dove with dark bars on the breast and back, and bare blue skin around the eye. Its call is a rapid series of short, high-pitched 'coos.' Native to Southeast Asia but widely introduced to islands such as Hawaii and Mauritius. Abundant in urban and suburban areas, and considered a common species.

A tiny bird with brownish upperparts and bright lemon-yellow underparts. Distinguished from sunbirds by its short, straight bill. Found in parks, forests, and plantations, often moving in mixed flocks through the canopy. Generally regarded as a common species.

A shy brown bird that creeps through leaf litter with a mouse-like movement. It has a gray face, rusty-tinged wings, and a scaly-headed look from dark-edged pale feathers. Found in mid- to high-altitude forests across its range. Generally considered a common but secretive species.

A small, chunky brown bird with pale eyebrows, heavy streaking, and a contrasting pale throat. It lives in dense montane forest ravines and streams, often hard to spot but sometimes inquisitive. Its song is a series of high, clear piping whistles. Generally regarded as a common species at higher elevations.

A large, flightless rail 33-40 cm long, with dark slate-grey plumage, brown back and wings, and bright red bare skin around the eyes, bill, and legs. Endemic to Halmahera Island in North Maluku, Indonesia, it inhabits dense sago swamps adjacent to forest and is rarely seen due to its shy nature and impenetrable habitat. Its presence is usually detected by a distinctive low drumming call accompanied by wing-beating rather than by sight. The population is estimated at 3,500-15,000 individuals.

A striking partridge with males showing blackish bodies and bright crimson heads, necks, and breasts. Females are duller with brownish-black plumage and orangish-red on the head and chest, while juveniles have crimson only on the crown. Endemic to Borneo, it inhabits lower montane forests between 1,000–1,700 m, occasionally ranging lower or higher. Considered a generally uncommon species but not globally threatened.

A dark rusty-orange kingfisher with a thick red bill and a bright azure rump, most visible in flight. It inhabits shaded waterways in dense forests and is shy, rarely seen in the open. On breeding grounds it gives a loud descending rattle, while wintering birds remain silent. Generally regarded as an uncommon species.

A chunky, slow-moving bird of lowland and foothill tropical forests. Males have a black head with crimson underparts and a pinkish nape patch, while females are brown-headed with a bold white collar above the red breast. Often seen perched quietly at mid-levels, usually in pairs. Considered a common species.

A trogon with males showing brilliant scarlet rumps and underparts, while females have orangish rumps, yellow breasts, and pinkish-red bellies. Both sexes share dark heads, barred wings, and a blue gape patch, with the female’s head paler. It inhabits evergreen lowland to montane forests, swamps, and foothills across Southeast Asia. Currently considered a near-threatened species due to habitat loss.

A striking trogon of lowland and foothill forests, often sluggish and hard to detect. Males have a black head, red nape, and bright red underparts with barred black-and-white wings, while females are brownish with yellow underparts. Both sexes show bright blue eye-rings and bills. Considered a common species.

A medium-sized (25-31 cm), sedentary trogon of the lower canopy in lowland and montane forest from southern China through Southeast Asia to Borneo, Sumatra, and Java. Males have an olive-yellow head, cinnamon-brown/rufous-chestnut upperparts, and a yellow-orange breast and belly; females are duller with grey-brown heads and upperparts. Both sexes have blue eye-rings and hunt insects using a sally-and-stall technique from a perch.

A medium-sized bird with long tail streamers and a dark greenish-blue crest. Males have rufous faces, while females show a dark mask; both share pale gray upperparts and underparts with a whitish belly. It favors lightly wooded habitats and avoids open plains or dense wet forests. Generally considered a common species.

A large long-tailed cuckoo with a raptor-like look, dark gray back, rufous chest, and barred white belly; juveniles are browner with streaked throats. Often perches high in tall trees and is best detected by its piercing 'wit-CHEW-WEE' call. Inhabits montane and foothill forests. Considered a common species.

A large hawk-like cuckoo with gray upperparts, streaked white underparts, and a long barred tail; juveniles are darker brown. It forages high in dense forest canopies and is often difficult to see. Similar to the Large Hawk-Cuckoo and Hodgson’s Hawk-Cuckoo but differs in plumage details. Currently regarded as a common species.
The Sunda bush warbler is a species of bird in the family Cettiidae. It is found in Indonesia, Malaysia, and the Philippines.

A small, gem-like pitta of Borneo’s lowland dipterocarp forests, found in both pristine and logged areas. Males show an electric-blue crown, dark blue underparts, white throat, and cinnamon-brown back, while females have a cinnamon head with paler brown underparts. Both sexes have dark wings with a white streak. Considered a near-threatened species due to habitat loss.

A colorful pitta with bold black, yellow, and chestnut barring, and a distinctive blue tail. It inhabits forests of Thailand, the Malay Peninsula, and Sumatra. Previously grouped with the Bornean and Javan banded pittas as a single species but now recognized as distinct. Currently considered a near-threatened species due to habitat loss.

A striking pitta with a bright yellow eyebrow, black mask, and barred underparts; males have an indigo belly patch and yellow throat, while females are duller with a whitish throat. Found in hill and lowland dipterocarp forests, often favoring dry ridges and limestone areas. Its song is a mellow, explosive 'byow!' given at intervals. Considered uncommon to locally common.

A slim, agile flycatcher from southern and southeastern Asia. Males show a black nape patch and half collar, while females are duller with olive-brown wings and no head markings. Found in tropical forests, often in pairs and sometimes joining mixed-species flocks. Considered a common species, though plumage and size vary slightly across populations.

A medium-sized (24-27 cm) arboreal songbird found from the Western Ghats of India through Southeast Asia to Sumatra, Java, and Borneo. Males are glossy, iridescent dark blue above with black underparts and wings and crimson eyes; females and first-year males are dull blue-green overall. It is social, moving in pairs or small parties through evergreen and humid forest from lowlands to about 1,600 m, feeding mainly on fruit and figs.

A dull brown bulbul, paler below with a thin orange eyering around its red eye. It shows a gray-washed head and breast contrasting with rufous-brown wings and rump, separating it from Cream-vented and Red-eyed Bulbuls. Found in lowland and foothill forests, usually in more mature growth. Generally considered a common species.

A large olive-brown bulbul with a long chisel-like bill, pale gray underparts, and white streaking on the throat and breast. Similar to Buff-vented Bulbul but lacks the yellow vent and shows clear streaking. Found in lowland and foothill forests, foraging in middle and upper levels, often with mixed flocks. Generally considered a common species.

A brown-and-white terrestrial babbler that moves like a rodent through leaf litter. It has a speckled black crown, white face and underparts, and a rusty-orange forehead. Found in wetter lowland to submontane forests. Generally considered a common species.

A small sunbird with a distinctive purple nape, the sole member of its genus. Found from Myanmar to Sumatra and Borneo. Inhabits subtropical and tropical moist lowland forests. Generally considered a common species.

A striking tree kingfisher and the sole member of its genus. Males have a bright blue crown with black-and-blue banding, while females show rufous-and-black banding on the head and upperparts. Found in lowland tropical forests of Southeast Asia. Generally considered a common species.

A small sunbird with a long downcurved bill and marked sexual dimorphism. Males are mostly blackish with iridescent crown, rump, and tail, plus a reddish-purple to bluish throat depending on region. Females are plainer, with grayish head, olive upperparts, and yellowish underparts; juveniles resemble females but with yellower throats. Found in forest edges, woodlands, gardens, and scrub. Generally considered a common species.

A small olive-brown songbird, sparrow-sized with a downcurved bill, chest speckling, and a small white cheek patch; juveniles instead show bare pink cheek skin. It lives in noisy groups across forests, mangroves, scrub, and cultivated areas of the eastern Lesser Sundas and Tanimbar Islands. Distinguished from the Indonesian Honeyeater by its breast speckling and facial mark. Generally considered a common species.

A sparrow-sized dark warbler with chestnut-brown upperparts, whitish eyebrow, white throat, and dull gray underparts, often with a scruffy tail. Once grouped with the Buru and Seram Bush Warblers but now recognized as distinct. It lives secretively near the ground in montane and foothill forests. Generally considered a common species.

A small, secretive brown warbler with pale underparts and faint streaking on the throat and chest. Endemic to Java, Bali, Timor, and Alor, it inhabits dense grasses and bushes at montane forest edges. More often heard than seen, with a song of buzzy notes. Generally considered a common species.

A large crow-like bird-of-paradise, mostly blackish with paler brown wings; juveniles appear browner overall. It has a thin neck, small head, and prominent brow ridges, distinguishing it from crows. Endemic to Halmahera and nearby islands, inhabiting forest canopies, edges, and plantations in lowlands and hills. Generally considered a common species.

A handsome babbler with a chestnut crown, black throat, and bright blue facial skin patches. It forages in dense lower vegetation of lowland and hill forests. Its vocalizations include quiet hooting notes, along with churrs, rattles, and croaks. Generally considered a common species.

A large long-tailed brown dove with pale head and underparts, and darker rufous-brown upperparts. Males often show a pale nape patch with little barring, while females have heavy neck barring. Found from coastal forests to mountains, in pairs or small groups. Generally considered a common species.

A small brown dove with an unusually long, broad tail. Both sexes have dark brown upperparts and orange-brown underparts, with females showing black chest spots absent in males. Prefers dense undergrowth of broadleaf evergreen forests and is often elusive. Generally considered a common species.

A plain sandy-brown babbler with orange-tan sides, a grayish face, and a heavy hooked bill. It inhabits degraded and secondary forests, foraging slowly near the ground, usually alone or in pairs. Its calls include churrs and whistles, with a song of 3–4 melodic notes. Generally considered a common species.

A small plain babbler with a short tail, brown body, gray head, and pale underparts. It shows a dark gray crown, faint breast streaks, and buffy flanks, distinguishing it from Abbott’s Babbler. Found in lowland and foothill forests, with a song of sharp chips followed by whistles. Generally considered a common species.

A medium-sized plain babbler with a gray face, dark gray crown, faint brown chest wash, and a dark rufous tail, giving it a bulbul-like outline. It inhabits open forests, edges, clearings, and riparian brush in lowlands and foothills. Similar to Moustached and Scaly-crowned babblers but lacks their distinctive markings. Generally considered a common species.

A small, dull brown babbler with pale underparts and a faint orangish scaled crown, distinguishing it from the darker-crowned Sooty-capped Babbler. Northern populations show a chestnut front crown and dark rear crown. It inhabits lowland and foothill forests, often in wetter areas, foraging in noisy flocks. Generally considered a common species.

A dull gray-brown babbler with a white belly and a faint moustache, often hard to see. Very similar to the Sooty-capped Babbler but distinguished by its paler crown and faint facial markings. Found in lowland and hill forests, foraging in small flocks at middle and lower levels. Generally considered a common species

A brown babbler with a pale belly, reddish eyes, and a bicolored crown—rusty red in front and black at the back. It shows a rufous forecrown and faint gray streaking on the breast, larger than the similar Scaly-crowned Babbler. Found in lowland forests of mainland Southeast Asia and mid-elevation areas of Sumatra and Borneo, often in mixed flocks. Currently considered a near-threatened species due to habitat loss.

A medium-sized, bulbul-like songbird with olive-green upperparts, yellowish head and chest, and a pinkish-brown bill. Central and southeast Sulawesi birds show rufous wings and tail, while others are olive. Endemic to Sulawesi, it lives in montane forests, often noisy in pairs or groups that lead mixed-species flocks. Generally considered a common species.

A medium-sized blackish bird (~41 cm) with a short crest, bare red face, and dark legs; males and females look alike. Found in forests, swamps, and mangroves of the Maluku and Raja Ampat Islands. It lays eggs in large mounds of earth, leaves, and sand, sometimes over 10 m wide. Generally considered a common species.

A small (~17 cm), rare woodpecker endemic to Java, Indonesia. Males show close black-and-white barring/striping with a whitish lower back/rump; females lack reddish facial markings, and juveniles are darker overall. It forages quietly through tree canopies and small branches in primary and secondary forest and forest edge, often joining mixed-species flocks. Also called Zebra Woodpecker; note this species was formerly lumped with the more widespread, non-endemic Buff-rumped Woodpecker (Meiglyptes grammithorax), which is now treated as a separate species.

A medium-sized woodpecker with a buff-colored neck, found in Brunei, Indonesia, Malaysia, Myanmar, Singapore, and Thailand. It inhabits subtropical and tropical moist lowland forests as well as swamps. The species is currently threatened by habitat loss. Status: Langka.

A dark laughingthrush with a bright orange-red bill and a patch of bare bluish-gray skin behind the eye. Found in foothill and submontane forests of the Thai-Malay Peninsula and Sumatra, often in pairs or small flocks. Its call is a series of loud, high-pitched bubbling clucks. Currently considered a common species.

A medium-sized brown woodpecker with a short bill, native to South and Southeast Asia. It forages in pairs, feeding mainly on ants and termites in scrub, evergreen, and deciduous forests. Uniquely nests inside the carton nests of arboreal ants (Crematogaster).

A small, noisy babbler with bright chestnut upperparts, blue-gray face, pale eyes, and streaked white underparts. Usually in groups, it inhabits lowland and hill forests, including secondary growth, scrub, and plantations. Active in the undergrowth, giving loud churring calls and echoey duet songs.

A small yellowish-brown babbler with striped underparts, rufous forehead, and wings. It forages in the undergrowth and mid-levels of forests, often joining mixed flocks. Vocalizations include harsh chatters and a bouncing monotone song of hollow pings.

The largest living woodpecker, gray overall with a pinkish cheek blush and pale orange throat patch. Long-necked and gangly, giving it a stretched appearance. Found in pristine forests with tall trees, often in small family flocks

A long-tailed, slender flycatcher with a fine bill. Males are entirely glossy blue-black, while females are chestnut above and white below with a black crown and face. Found in mangroves and moist lowland forests from northern Australia to the Moluccas and Bismarck Archipelago. Status: Common.

A tiny skulking babbler with a short tail and large head, brown overall with a pale throat, bold white eyebrow, and white speckling above. Both upperparts and underparts are streaked, with variation across its range. Found in dense undergrowth of evergreen forests from lowlands to submontane zones across South and Southeast Asia.

A brown hawk-like owl with piercing yellow eyes, a solid brown back, and a white breast with patchy brown spots. Found across South and Southeast Asia in habitats ranging from rainforests to orchards and gardens. Nocturnal, often hunting from exposed perches, and gives a series of rising hoots.

A small owl (about 23 cm, 90 g) endemic to the Indonesian island of Sumba, found in subtropical/tropical moist lowland primary and secondary forest. It has a grey head with fine dark barring, distinctive white eyebrows, an indistinct facial disc, a large yellow bill, yellow eyes, and no ear tufts; upperparts are light brown with dark wavy markings and underparts whitish with fine dark chevrons. Formally described in 2002; also called Least Boobook in some checklists (Clements/eBird, BirdLife/HBW).

A large forest-dwelling raptor with highly variable plumage, ranging from pale to dark, giving it the name 'changeable.' It has a long crest, feathered tarsi, and powerful build, preying on birds, mammals, and reptiles. Found widely across South and Southeast Asia in diverse habitats, showing strong adaptability.

A large, unmistakable leaf-green bee-eater with a red 'beard' extending from crown to belly. It hunts flying insects from perches in mid to upper forest levels. Found in lowland and foothill forests of Southeast Asia, often in forest openings.

A small, secretive warbler with rusty underparts, gray-brown back, blackish crown front, and a white chin and throat. Found in lowland and montane forests of Indonesia and Papua New Guinea, often in the understory. More often heard than seen, giving a high-pitched, whistled song.

A streak-breasted oriole with a long pink bill and dark red eyes. Males are yellow with a black hood and dark wings, while females are olive-green above. Found in lowland and hill forests of Southeast Asia, replaced by Ventriloquial Oriole in northern Borneo. Usually forages in upper forest levels, singly or in pairs.

A bright tailorbird with olive upperparts, pale underparts, and a rufous crown. Males show a distinctive dark neck patch, though it can be hard to see. Found in lowland forests, edges, and gardens, usually staying hidden in dense vegetation. Its song is a series of trilled phrases, often in duets, with sharp nasal calls.

A small, bright tailorbird with olive upperparts, rufous crown, and pale underparts. It inhabits lowland forests and mangroves across Southeast Asia, including Brunei, Indonesia, Malaysia, and the Philippines. Usually active in dense vegetation, often giving sharp calls.

A small, active tailorbird with a grayish body, orange face, and long thin bill, often holding its tail cocked. Both sexes look similar, though females are paler with a whiter throat. Found in lowland and foothill edges, gardens, scrub, and secondary growth across Southeast Asia. Very similar to Ashy Tailorbird but shows faint olive wash above and yellowish belly tinge.

A small tailorbird with olive upperparts, pale underparts, and a distinctive rufous tail. Found in forests and mangroves across Southeast Asia, including Brunei, Indonesia, Malaysia, and the Philippines. Often active in dense vegetation, moving singly or in pairs with sharp calls.

A small songbird with a long upright tail, greenish upperparts, and a rust-colored crown and forehead. Common across tropical Asia in gardens, scrub, farmland, and forest edges, often betrayed by its loud calls despite staying hidden in vegetation. Famous for sewing leaves together with plant fibers or spider silk to build its nest.

A small owl (19-21 cm) endemic to Flores, Indonesia, with yellow eyes, a pale/orange-yellow bill, reddish rictal bristles, short rounded ear-tufts, white vermiculation on the forehead, dark rufous neck feathers with white bases, and white scapular spots. It inhabits montane forest at roughly 1,000-1,400 m elevation (recent records also show it in drier monsoon forest at lower altitudes). Its distinctive call is a rapid, staccato series delivered in bouts up to several minutes long.

A small brown scops-owl with large dark eyes and short ear tufts. Distinguished from similar scops-owls by its nearly black eyes and overall coloration. Found in forests, gardens, plantations, and parks across the Sunda Islands. More often heard than seen, giving yelping hoots at intervals.

description: A small scops-owl with ear tufts, yellow eyes, bold streaked underparts, and patterned upperparts with white wing marks. Found in forests and plantations of the Maluku and Lesser Sunda Islands. Best told from similar species by its dark crown and frog-like croaking call

A small owl with dark red eyes, soft ear tufts, and lightly speckled underparts. Found in lowland and foothill forests of Southeast Asia. More often heard than seen, giving quiet whistled hoots at intervals.

A small brown owl of montane broadleaf forests, dark above and pale below, with bright yellow eyes. Shows frosty white brows and a contrasting white stripe on the back, with shorter ear tufts than Collared Scops-Owl. Gives long series of soft double hoots at intervals.

A small, drab bird with brown-gray upperparts and white underparts, lacking strong markings. Found mainly in mangrove forests but also in nearby vegetation. Song consists of ringing or complex whistled phrases depending on region.

A drab, unobtrusive bird (15.5-17 cm) with a thick black bill, slaty-grey crown, brown upperparts, and white underparts with a greyish-washed breast and duller throat. It inhabits mangroves and adjacent well-vegetated habitats (coastal scrub, plantations, wooded gardens, swamp forest) across South and Southeast Asia, and typically sits still for long periods among foliage, more often heard than seen. Note: Pachycephala grisola is a taxonomic synonym used by older checklists (IOC v1.0-1.7, Clements through ~2010); current authorities use Pachycephala cinerea for the same species.

The Bornean whistler. The upperparts are dark olive and the underparts yellow, with males usually brighter than females. Found in the central highland forests of Borneo, foraging at middle levels and joining mixed flocks. Still fairly common in its natural range and often detected by its loud, bright song.

The Sulphur-vented whistler. A sparrow-sized songbird with a thick bill, rich brown upperparts, and whitish underparts washed yellow on the lower belly and undertail. Juveniles show a gray head and rufous tones in the plumage. Found in the forested foothills and mountains of Sulawesi, often in pairs within mixed flocks, and still fairly common in its restricted range.

The Cinereous tit. A robust songbird with a black head, white cheek, and a black stripe running down the white belly, with gray wings and sometimes a greenish tinge near the shoulder. Juveniles appear duller, replacing the black with gray. Found widely from India to Indonesia in habitats ranging from dense hill forests to city parks, and remains a very common and vocal species.

The Stork-billed kingfisher. A massive kingfisher with an olive-brown head, dark green-blue upperparts, and buff underparts, with bluer wings and tail and a large scarlet bill. Juveniles show dusky edges to the feathers of the breast and hindneck. Found along rivers, streams, and large water bodies with vegetated banks across South and Southeast Asia, and remains a widespread but often locally sparse resident.

The Ferruginous babbler. A reddish-brown bird with white underparts, warmer in tone than the similar White-chested babbler. Often forages in small flocks at the lower and middle levels of lowland and foothill forests. Found from Myanmar through Thailand to Borneo and Sumatra, and still fairly common in suitable habitats.

The Bornean black-capped babbler. A chunky brown babbler with a dark face and crown, white eyebrows, a bright white throat, and an orange breast. It scurries on the forest floor through leaf litter, either alone or in pairs. Endemic to Borneo, it inhabits lowland and foothill forests and remains regularly encountered in its natural range.

The Sulawesi babbler. A small, plump brown bird with long legs and pale whitish to buff underparts, plain in overall appearance. Usually found singly or in pairs on or near the ground in the understory of lowland and foothill forests and forest edges. Endemic to Sulawesi and nearby islands, it is the only babbler in its range and remains a common and vocal species.

The Short-tailed babbler. A long-legged brown babbler with a very short tail, pale underparts, and a gray face giving a slightly moustached look. It forages close to the ground in lowland and foothill evergreen forests, usually alone or in pairs. Found in Peninsular Malaysia, Singapore, Thailand, Sumatra, and Borneo, and currently assessed as Near Threatened.

The Malayan black-capped babbler. A chunky brown babbler with a dark crown, gray face, bright white throat, and an orange breast. It forages on the forest floor, rummaging through leaf litter alone or in pairs. Endemic to the Malay Peninsula and nearby islands including Sumatra, Bangka, Belitung, and Natuna, it inhabits lowland and foothill forests and remains regularly encountered in its range.

Temminck’s babbler. A small, warm brown babbler with a white throat, a dark crown, and a variable face color from gray to cinnamon-brown. It keeps to the shadowy forest floor and dense undergrowth of lowland and foothill forests, often hard to spot. Found in Borneo and Java, it remains regularly encountered in suitable habitats.

A small brown bird with white underparts, duller and grayer than the similar Ferruginous babbler. It forages on the ground in wet lowland areas, especially along rivers and in coastal mangroves, and is usually seen in pairs. Endemic to specialized habitats, it remains localized within its range and is currently assessed as Near Threatened.

The Buff-breasted babbler. A brown babbler with long legs and a long tail, averaging paler below and nearly unmarked overall. It keeps close to the ground in foothill forests with dense undergrowth, often foraging quietly in cover. Widely distributed from South to Southeast Asia, it remains a common species throughout much of its range.

The Scarlet minivet. A brightly colored songbird with males ranging from scarlet to orange or yellow with black upperparts, while females are grayish with yellow underparts and a yellow forehead. Both sexes show distinctive blotches on the wings, unlike other minivets. Found across South and Southeast Asia in forests and wooded habitats, it forages high in the canopy, often in mixed flocks, and remains a common resident throughout its range.

The Helmeted friarbird. A large brownish honeyeater with bare black facial skin and a long pointed bill topped by a small rounded horn, darker above and paler below. It inhabits coastal northern Australia from Queensland to the Northern Territory in habitats ranging from mangroves and forests to gardens and parks, with one subspecies adapted to inland sandstone areas. Widespread and often common, it is noisy and conspicuous, giving a range of harsh, loud calls.

The Rufous-winged philentoma. An ambiguous-looking forest bird resembling a mix of monarch and paradise-flycatcher. Males show either rufous wings with a white belly or are entirely gray-blue, while females are grayish above with rufous wings and tail. It inhabits lowland and hill forests from Myanmar to Borneo, hunting from perches at lower to middle levels. Its song is a short pair of whistled notes, often with harsh chattering calls.

The Maroon-breasted philentoma. A slender forest bird with dark slaty-blue plumage, black face, and a maroon breast patch in males, while females lack the patch but show a dark throat. Found in lowland and foothill forests, it forages quietly at middle levels and gives clear whistled songs alongside harsh churring calls. Classified as Near Threatened.

The Oriental bay owl. A small nocturnal owl with a highly angular head and a heart-shaped facial disc marked by short rounded tufts. Chestnut-brown above and pale grayish below with light spotting on the sides, its dark eyes stand out against the pale face. Found in evergreen forests, mangroves, and other wooded habitats across South and Southeast Asia, it remains locally common though often difficult to see.

The Mountain tailorbird. A tiny warbler with a long bill and a frequently cocked tail, green above and yellow below with a pale gray throat and bright orange forehead. It stays low in the brushy undergrowth of hill and submontane forest, often solitary but sometimes in small mixed flocks. Widely distributed across South and Southeast Asia, it remains a fairly common bird within its range.

The Yellow-breasted warbler. A small, brightly colored warbler with an orange-brown head, white eye-ring, and yellow underparts. It inhabits montane and submontane forests on Sumatra, Borneo, and Palawan, usually foraging in mixed flocks. The species remains fairly common in suitable forest habitats across its range.

The Island leaf warbler. A small warbler with a pale eyebrow, dull greenish upperparts, white throat, and yellowish underparts, sometimes showing a faint central crown stripe. It inhabits lowland and hill forests across the Moluccas to the Solomon Islands, often joining mixed-species flocks. The species remains fairly common throughout its range, despite small variations among populations.

The Mountain leaf warbler. A small leaf warbler with a patterned head, including a black eye-stripe, pale eyebrow, and central crown stripe; upperparts yellowish green and underparts bright yellow. It inhabits montane rainforests throughout Sundaland, often foraging in the upper forest levels. The species remains fairly common across its range.

The Crimson-winged woodpecker. A medium-sized woodpecker with greenish body, bright red wings and crown, yellow crest, cream-speckled sides, and pale blue-gray “spectacles”; males have a greener face and a maroon mustache stripe. It inhabits primary and secondary forests, lowlands to lower montane areas, as well as plantations and logged tracts, often foraging on trunks and large branches in pairs or mixed flocks. The species remains regularly encountered throughout its range in Southeast Asia.

Refers to the same species as Hydrornis irena (see that entry) under an older, broader taxonomy -- training data folder names for this label reference "ssp. irenae," the exact subspecies later split out as Hydrornis irena. A colorful pitta with bold black, yellow, and chestnut barring and a distinctive blue tail, inhabiting forests of Thailand, the Malay Peninsula, and Sumatra.

The Ivory-breasted pitta. A large, plump, short-tailed, long-legged forest bird with black upperparts, white underparts, a red lower belly, and a silvery-blue shoulder patch; juveniles are duller with buff underparts. It inhabits humid lowland and hill forests in North Maluku, Indonesia, usually found singly or in pairs and foraging on the ground. The species remains regularly encountered within its limited range.

The Hooded pitta. A striking forest bird with an emerald-green body, black head, ruby-red lower belly, powder-blue wing patch, and a warm brown crown stripe in birds from the Himalayas eastward. It inhabits a variety of wooded habitats from deep forests to plantations and can occasionally turn up in urban parks. The species remains fairly common and widespread throughout much of its range.

description: The Bornean bristlehead. A unique forest passerine with an incandescent head and dark, heavy hooked bill, giving it a top-heavy appearance. It inhabits the canopy of lowland and foothill primary dipterocarp and peatswamp forests in Borneo, often seen in small flocks and vocal with chattering notes and nasal whistles. The species is classified as Vulnerable and remains uncommon within its restricted range.

The Bornean black magpie. A glossy black treepie with a long tail, heavy hooked bill, and a small crest tuft. It inhabits lowland and foothill forests in Borneo, often moving in pairs or flocks, sometimes joining mixed-species groups. The species remains fairly common within its restricted range.

The Pygmy cupwing. A tiny, round brown bird with dark upperparts and black-and-white scaled underparts, giving it a furtive, rodent-like appearance. It inhabits subtropical and tropical moist lowland and montane forests from the Himalayas to the Lesser Sunda Islands. The species remains fairly common throughout its range.

The Black-sided robin. A medium-sized robin with black upperparts, head, and side chest markings, white underparts, thin white eyebrow, and wing patch. It inhabits lowland and foothill forests in Indonesia and Papua New Guinea, usually shy and more often heard than seen. The species remains fairly common within its range.

The Malayan peacock-pheasant. A stumpy, odd-looking pheasant with dull brown plumage, iridescent green and blue eye-spots on the upperparts, and a forward-pointing crest; males are thicker-set with brighter red facial skin. It inhabits lowland rainforests in the Malay Peninsula, moving quietly and displaying complex behaviors during the breeding season. The species is classified as Endangered and remains uncommon within its range.

A medium-sized pheasant up to 50 cm long, rufous-brown and black spotted, with an elongated crest and bare red skin around the eye. The male's breast sides are metallic blue-green bordering a white throat, and its tail feathers bear large blue-green ocelli fanned during courtship display. Endemic to Borneo, it inhabits lowland forest along river valleys between 100-500 m elevation, avoiding swampy areas. A very shy species that flees into cover rather than flying, it is rarely observed.

The Sunda scimitar babbler. An attractive babbler with a black head, long white eyebrow, white throat to mid-belly, dark maroon body, and slightly decurved horn-colored bill. It inhabits lowland and montane forests in Sumatra, Borneo, and Malaysia, usually foraging singly or in pairs and sometimes joining mixed-species flocks. The species remains fairly common within its range.

description: The Yellow-bellied prinia. A brightly colored prinia with dark upperparts, yellowish underparts, white throat, eyering, and a thin stripe between the eye and bill. It inhabits lowland fields, wetlands, and meadows across Pakistan, the southern Himalayan foothills, northeastern India, and Southeast Asia, usually found singly or in pairs. The species remains fairly common throughout its range.

The Flame-fronted barbet. A medium-sized barbet with green plumage, yellow-orange forehead, blue nape, and an orange chest crescent. It inhabits foothill and lower montane forests on Java and Bali, usually found from the lower slopes up to the base of the mountains. The species remains fairly common within its limited range.

The Golden-whiskered barbet. A green barbet with bright yellow cheeks, traces of red and blue on the head, and a heavy bill. It inhabits lowland and foothill forests of the western Malay Archipelago, foraging high in the canopy. The species remains fairly common and widespread throughout its range.

The Black-eared barbet. A small barbet of dense broad-leaved forests with bright green plumage and a blue face patterned with black, red, orange, and yellow; juveniles are green with a powder-blue face and no dark markings. It inhabits lowland and foothill forests in Peninsular Malaysia, Sumatra, and Borneo, usually staying in the dense canopy. The species remains fairly common throughout its wide range.

The Yellow-crowned barbet. A beautifully colored barbet with a powder-blue throat, dark eye mask, bright yellow forehead and brow, blue crown, and traces of red on the nape. It inhabits lowland forests and swamps in Brunei, Indonesia, Malaysia, Singapore, and Thailand. The species is classified as Near Threatened.

The Red-throated barbet. A large emerald-green barbet with a heavy dark bill; males have yellow, black, blue, and red facial patches, while females show only blue and red. It inhabits lowland forests, swamps, and wooded areas including parks and gardens in Brunei, Indonesia, Malaysia, Myanmar, Singapore, and Thailand, usually staying in the canopy. The species is classified as Near Threatened.

The Black-browed barbet. A loud but sluggish barbet with a leaf-green body, brightly colored face, and heavy black bill. It inhabits highland forests of Peninsular Malaysia and Sumatra, usually staying in the canopy and more often heard than seen. The species remains fairly common throughout its range.

The Golden-naped barbet. A stunning emerald-green barbet with a neon powder-blue crown and throat, and a golden nape visible from certain angles. It inhabits montane forests on Borneo, usually found from mid-elevation slopes up to high mountains, foraging in the middle and upper canopy. The species remains fairly common within its limited range.

The Fire-tufted barbet. A beautiful emerald-green barbet with silvery cheeks, white forehead, black chinstrap, and a small reddish tuft above the base of the bill; bill is horn-colored with a dark central smudge. It inhabits lowland and montane forests of Peninsular Malaysia and Sumatra, usually staying in the canopy. The species remains fairly common throughout its range.

The Red-crowned barbet. A brightly colored barbet with a massive dark bill, green body, and an intricate black-yellow-red-blue facial pattern; the entirely red crown distinguishes it from similar species. It inhabits wet lowland forests and plantations in Brunei, Indonesia, Malaysia, Myanmar, Singapore, and Thailand, usually staying in the upper canopy. The species is classified as Near Threatened.

The Chestnut-hooded laughingthrush. An attractive laughingthrush with a dark chestnut head, gray body, bright orange bill, silvery wing stripe, and a pale crescent behind the eye; juveniles are duller than adults. It inhabits montane forests and forest edges in Borneo’s highlands, often found near lodges and trails. The species remains fairly common within its restricted range.

White-browed Shrike-Babbler. A small, stout bird with a large head; males are black above with a gray back, white below, white eyebrow, and bright orange wing stripes, while females are grayer with yellow-green replacing the orange. It inhabits foothill and montane forests across the eastern Himalayas and Southeast Asia, often joining mixed-species flocks at mid-levels. The species remains fairly common throughout its range.

A large, stout wren-babbler with a whitish face, brown back, and white-and-black streaked underparts giving it a pot-bellied appearance. Endemic to Borneo (found in Brunei, Indonesia, and Malaysia), it depends on pristine lowland forest and swampland with a patchy distribution, foraging quietly under dense cover and fallen trunks, usually in pairs or small groups. It is shy and best detected by its song, a simple two-note whistle.

The Yellow-vented bulbul. An attractive brown bird above and whitish below, with a bright yellow vent, thick black line from bill to eye, and a weak dark-edged crest. It inhabits open habitats, cities, parks, plantations, forest edges, and secondary growth across Southeast Asia. The species remains very common throughout its range.

The Olive-winged bulbul. A large, plain brown bulbul with gray-streaked ear coverts, red eyes, and olive-green panels on the wings. It inhabits lowland forests, forest edges, and mangroves across southeastern Asia and the Greater Sunda Islands. The species remains fairly common throughout its range.
